differential.evidence_basis: Central facial erythema with a background of trigger-provoked flushing (heat/alcohol/sun) and an acute crop of papulopustules — the classic rosacea constellation.

Painful, burning, red, pustular face raised by telephone triage — must be actively excluded; excluded here by bilateral symmetry, absence of fever/systemic upset, and no indurated sharply-demarcated spreading border.
Inflammatory papulopustules can mimic acne — but the complete absence of comedones, the older age of onset and the background telangiectatic erythema point away from acne.
Central facial (malar) erythema raises SLE — but the classic SLE malar rash is a flat/raised plaque WITHOUT pustules, and no systemic features are reported.
Systemically well, bilateral symmetrical central erythema, papulopustules without comedones, prominent telangiectasia and no induration — diagnostic of a rosacea flare.
Afebrile and systemically well, bilateral and symmetrical, no sharply demarcated indurated spreading border and no lymphadenopathy — cellulitis effectively excluded on the patient-obtainable data.
No comedones and a background of persistent central erythema and telangiectasia — not acne.

Benign inflammatory condition; the pustules are sterile, not infective. Correct management is barrier repair, trigger avoidance and anti-inflammatory therapy, NOT antibacterial treatment of a presumed infection or ED escalation.

red_flags.description: Ocular rosacea — eye redness, grittiness/foreign-body sensation, watering or visual change
red_flags.clinical_significance: Ocular rosacea can cause keratitis and, untreated, corneal damage and sight threat — it is screened negative at presentation but is the key safety-net trigger for urgent review if it develops.

red_flags.description: Features of true facial cellulitis / spreading soft-tissue infection — fever, rigors, a hard hot sharply-demarcated spreading border, unilateral rapid progression, induration
red_flags.clinical_significance: The important-not-to-miss mimic; screened negative here (afebrile, bilateral, non-indurated, non-spreading) but would demand same-day in-person assessment and antibiotics if it emerged.

red_flags.description: Periocular/eyelid spread with pain or swelling, or hard hot facial swelling with fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise periorbital/orbital cellulitis or evolving facial cellulitis — an emergency (ED) trigger if it develops.

pathophysiology_summary: Rosacea is a chronic inflammatory disorder of the central face driven by dysregulated innate immunity (elevated cathelicidin/kallikrein-5), neurovascular hyper-reactivity and, in the papulopustular subtype, a brisk perifollicular inflammatory response (with a contributory role for Demodex mites). Vasodilatory triggers — heat, ultraviolet light, alcohol, spicy food — provoke flushing and, in a flare, an acute crop of sterile inflammatory papules and pustules on a background of fixed erythema and telangiectasia. Crucially the pustules are STERILE and inflammatory, not infective — hence sub-antimicrobial-dose oral tetracyclines (e.g. doxycycline 40-50 mg) work through anti-inflammatory rather than antibacterial mechanisms. A compromised epidermal barrier from harsh exfoliants and acids amplifies stinging and inflammation. Rosacea cannot be cured but is well controlled with trigger avoidance, gentle barrier-restoring skincare, photoprotection and topical/oral anti-inflammatory therapy.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by the patient's fear and the alarming pustular appearance into diagnosing facial cellulitis or a staphylococcal infection and escalating to ED — when the bilateral symmetry, afebrile state and absence of induration point to a benign rosacea flare.

Labelling the papulopustules as acne and reaching for comedolytics or benzoyl peroxide — irritant on a compromised rosacea barrier — when the absence of comedones and the erythrotelangiectatic background exclude acne.
Prescribing a topical steroid for the 'inflamed red rash' — steroids transiently soothe but drive steroid-induced/peri-oral rosacea and worsen the condition; they are contraindicated.

Treating the sterile pustules with an anti-staphylococcal antibiotic (e.g. flucloxacillin) as though they were infective — the pustules are sterile; the tetracycline used in rosacea is for its anti-inflammatory effect at sub-antimicrobial dose.
Failing to screen for and safety-net ocular rosacea — the one component that can threaten sight if it develops and is not reviewed.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation for induration, warmth gradient and true border demarcation of the erythema
Confirmation of absence of regional lymphadenopathy
Fine morphological confirmation (comedones vs pustules) beyond video resolution
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: None to emergency services. Recognise the likely benign papulopustular rosacea flare from video-observable and patient-reported data; give barrier-repair and trigger-avoidance self-care; arrange a routine GP (or telehealth-prescriber) review to consider sub-antimicrobial-dose oral doxycycline and topical anti-inflammatory therapy; and safety-net explicitly for ocular involvement and for evolving true cellulitis.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign diagnosis given the classic constellation. The dominant risk direction is OVER-action — mislabelling a benign flare as facial cellulitis/infection and over-triaging to ED, or prescribing a topical steroid / anti-staphylococcal antibiotic. The residual (small) under-call risk is missing an evolving cellulitis or ocular rosacea, which the safety-net edges cover.
